context_relevancy.query: List the classifications of controlled environments in aerospace facilities and specify the contamination control criteria for each classification.
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 3 TYPES OF ENVIRONMENTALLY CONTROLLED FACILITIES
There are three basic types of permanent environmen- tally controlled facilities that are utilized by the Air Force to achieve contamination control while working on systems hardware
They are, in order of cleanness:
(1) Controlled areas (Class 300, 000) Chapter 4
(2) Conventional Clean Rooms (Class 100, 000) Chapter 5
(3) Laminar Flow Rooms Chapter 6
The fourth type of environmentally controlled facili- ties consists of the various types of portable and semi- portable laminar flow devices
Laminar Flow Devices Chapter 7 (1) Class 1,000
Design and construction criteria for each type of environ- mentally controlled facility is contained in AF Manual 88-4, Chapter 5, "Criteria for Air Force Clean Facility Design and Constructions
For the same initial cost, contamination control can better be achieved by constructing a laminar flow room
The laminar flow clean rooms have proven their worth in both economy of operation and positive environmental con- trol, and every effort should be made toward this type of design
Where laminar flow clean rooms are not practical, consideration should be given to using laminar flow devices within a controlled area (Class 300,000)
ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROL REQUIRE- MENTS
Table 3-1 specifies the minimum standards required for each type of environmentally controlled facility
1 NATURE AND CHARACTERISTICS OF PAR- TICULATE CONTAMINATION
should be kept in mind that "Clean" is that contamination level just below that which affects the operation or reliability of the product, and specifying cleanliness above necessary levels is costly
Contamination is any substance, material or energy, that ad- versely affects Air Force system hardware
It illustrates typical characteristics of particulate contamination
In view of today's technological developments and manufacturing advances in the area of mechanical, electronic, electromechanical, optical, electro- optic, computer, photographic, hydraulic, and pneumatic sys- tems, the need for contamination control is unquestioned
Contamination is the single cause of many Air Force system and component failures daily
3 OTHER ENVIRONMENTAL DOCUMENTS
1 AF Manual 88-4, Chapter 5
This manual pre- scribes criteria for design and construction of Air Force clean room facilities
It specifies the real property standards for meeting the requirements of this technical order
ISO 14644-1 and ISO 14644-2 refer to the classes of environmental areas and certain air conditioning requirements
IEST-STD-CC1246 is to be used by all branches of the military services when specifying product cleanliness

context_relevancy.query: Airflow design considerations for Class 100 cleanrooms in aerospace facilities
context_chunks:
(2) Conventional Clean Rooms (Class 100, 000) Chapter 5
(3) Laminar Flow Rooms Chapter 6
For the same initial cost, contamination control can better be achieved by constructing a laminar flow room
The laminar flow clean rooms have proven their worth in both economy of operation and positive environmental con- trol, and every effort should be made toward this type of design
Where laminar flow clean rooms are not practical, consideration should be given to using laminar flow devices within a controlled area (Class 300,000)
CHAPTER 6 LAMINAR FLOW CLEAN ROOMS CROSS FLOW (CLASS 10,000) AND DOWN FLOW (CLASS 1,000)
A typical cross-flow room is shown in Figure 6-1
Some degree of air cleanliness will be sacrificed toward the exhaust end of the room
Laminar flow clean rooms are the latest in clean room design and are second generation to conventional clean rooms
A higher level of cleanliness can be maintained in these type facilities because of the nature of laminar air flow patterns
Turbulent air flow patterns, which are characteristic of con- ventional clean rooms, tend to stir up and keep internally generated contamination in the air mass that lingers within the room
Laminar air flow patterns, which are essentially unidirectional as the air flows from the air supply to the exhaust, eliminate or substantially reduce the turbulent flow, and produce a cleaner air mass within the room
These types of clean rooms are generally classified as cross-flow (class 10,000) and down-flow (class 1,000), but are capable of be- ing maintained at class 100
When the air moves from ceiling to floor with the floor serving as the return air grille, the room is called a down-flow or vertical laminar flow clean room
The down-flow design provides the greatest contamination control over the entire working area because airborne con- tamination is immediately carried down and out of the room as soon as it is generated
per min) than the cross-flow room (100 ft
It will be possible to maintain a down-flow room at class 100 if required
Laminar flow clean work stations are designed to achieve the highest class cleanness (Class 100) specified in this Technical Order, but they may also be used to achieve Class 1,000
If a lami- nar flow work station is used to achieve a lesser clean con- dition than class 100, then it shall be labeled for that class and be monitored for the higher particulate count
Class 100 laminar flow clean work stations are normally located in conventional clean rooms, controlled areas, or laminar flow clean rooms as defined by this Technical Order
Class 1,000 or higher clean work stations are normally located in con- trolled areas or air conditioned rooms
If clean work stations are placed in air conditioned spaces, care must be exercised to assure that air flow patterns within the work station are not disturbed by turbulent air flow from outside areas when doors are opened and closed
4 Air Flow
The clean work station is enclosed on the sides and top to ensure laminar flow and prevent air currents from causing contamination of the workbench
The flow of air may be horizontal or vertical as long as the flow is essentially laminar and achieves the cleanliness level desired

context_relevancy.query: How do emergency shutdown procedures integrate with routine fuel servicing operations to ensure safety during aircraft refueling activities?
context_chunks:
EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN PROCEDURES
During aircraft servicing, the refueling unit operator will monitor the refueler for fuel leaks, ignition sources and other indications of a possible malfunction
In the event of an emergency, shutdown the unit as follows:
Notify aircraft servicing supervisor, operations expediter and Fuels Control Center (FCC), if possible
In case of fuel spill other than normal aircraft venting, do not evacuate fuel servicing vehicle until area is declared safe by fire department personnel
In the event of an emergency, shutdown the refueling unit as follows:
Notify aircraft fuel servicing supervisor and Fuels Control Center
Emergency Shutdown Procedures

(4) Notify aircraft servicing crew member, operations expediter, and Fuels Service Center (FSC)

During aircraft servicing, the R-18 Pumping Unit operator and R-20 Ser- vicing Platform operator will monitor the pumping unit and system area for fuel leaks, ignition sources and other indications of possible malfunc- tion

context_relevancy.query: Describe the key elements typically included in corrosion risk checklists used for Air Force equipment maintenance.
context_chunks:
REMIS is the AF Maintenance Enterprise system providing organizational, in- termediate, and depot-level operational authoritative information for all AF weapon systems (aircraft, Remote-Piloted Vehicles (RPV), Satellite, Missile, Trainer, Mine Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP) vehicles, Communications-Eeetrnniii (C-E), ICBM, Automatic Test Equipment (ATE), and Support Equipment (SE)), supporting AF world-wide operating bases and locations
REMIS is designed to accumulate data and pro- vide information necessary to support the AF equipment maintenance program outlined in DAFI 21-101
4 Reliability Asset Monitoring System
The Reliability Asset Monitoring (RAM) System is the AF MIS used to track serialized TCTO compliance, weapon configuration, inspections and other key maintenance data and Operational Safety, Suitability and Effectiveness (OSS&E) support of missiles/munitions listed in TO 21M-1-10
RAM is the official AF system of record for the following data: AF Weapon System mas- ter inventory, Weapon System maintenance data, Master Validation tables, Inventory Assignment, Capability Status, Flying Hours, TCTO Sta- tus, and Chief Financial Office (CFO) reporting
The ANS is the system of record for notifying senior leadership that Fleet Grounding Removal from Service/ Restricted Operations is imminent
Prior to issuing an Advance No- tification, the PM or their designee shall conduct a risk assessment in accordance with MIL-STD-882, to determine the level of technical risk associated with the deficiency (Chapter 9)
6 Maintenance Information Systems
MIS refers to automated maintenance information systems that support and enable maintenance business processes
MIS is used to document maintenance actions and track fleet health
Recommend specific System Program Director (SPD)/System Program Offices (SPO) in conjunc- tion with affected MAJCOM Corrosion Functional Managers to develop a detailed checklist for each weapon system
Standardized checklists for each phase of a deployment for aircraft and associated equipment will greatly improve the overall condi- tion when operating in SWA environments and in- crease equipment survivability and serviceability
Some general requirements in the absence of weapons systems guidance are included in Para- graph 9
2 PRE-DEPLOYMENT RECOMMENDATIONS
Inspect entire aircraft exterior for missing and chipped primer and/or paint
Inspect and replace any worn, torn or cracked seals and gaskets that will allow sand and dust intrusion
Accomplish a complete wash prior to deployment

context_relevancy.query: Where is the S-3's single point receptacle located?
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 6 S-3 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURE
1 S-3 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURE
The Single Point Receptacle (SPR) is located on the right side of the fuselage immediately aft of the main landing gear well (Figure 6-1)
Manual pre-check controls and the tank pressure gauge are located in the right main wheel well "around the corner" from the SPR
Internal tanks are vented through a single outlet at the aft end of the fuselage, while the external tanks are vented at the tank bottoms
A single fuel dump (jettison) chute is located at the fuselage aft end
1 Auxiliary Power Unit (APU)
The aircraft has an on board APU with an exhaust port on the fuselage left side (near the running engine)
The door has a link that operates a safing switch
CHAPTER 3 E-2 HOT REFUELING
1 E-2 HOT REFUELING
The Single Point Receptacle (SPR) and refueling control panel (Figure 3-1) are located on the inboard side of the right engine nacelle
The Single Point Receptacle (SPR) is located on the lower part of the right engine nacelle about 2
The fueling control panel (station) is immediately aft of the right nacelle boarding ladder at approximately eye level
Aircraft fuel dump outlets are located on the wing trailing edges near the wingtips and at the aft end of the fuselage
Fuel vent outlets are located in the aft fuselage and on the undersides of the wings near the wingtips
The A-6 aircraft SPR panel receives electrical power from the right engine only, so A-6 aircraft cannot be hot refueled at United States Air Force (USAF) bases until the entire fleet is modified to provide electrical power to the SPR panel from the left engine
EA-6 aircraft can be hot refueled with the right engine shut down because the left engine can provide electrical power to the SPR panel
Refueling will be accomplished using D-2 or D-3 straight throat nozzle(s) connected to Single Point Receptacle (SPR) on the wing opposite the operating engine
Aircraft/cquipment must be grounded and/or bonded prior to connecting the single point nozzle to the aircraft; however, the hydrant coupler will be connected to the hydrant outlet prior to bonding the hydrant-servicing vehicle to the aircraft
Ensure the crew member has opened the single point nozzle(s)
C-130 and C-17 aircraft troop doors and emergency hatches on the right or Single Point Receptacle (SPR) side of the aircraft must be closed during concurrent servicing operations to iso- late the cargo department from the fuel servicing safety zone
Aircraft/equipment must be grounded and/or bonded prior to connecting the single point nozzle to the aircraft; however, the hydrant coupler will be connected to the hydrant outlet prior to bonding the hydrant-servicing vehicle to the aircraft
Ensure the Concurrent Servicing Crew has opened the single point nozzle(s)

context_relevancy.query: What are the seven types of USAF TCTOs?
context_chunks:
When the CCB directs a modification to be accomplished by TCTO, the TCTO is developed and format- ted according to MIL-DTL-38804
TCTO verification is performed by a team of the same Air Force Specialty Code (AFSC) and skill level as those who will accomplish the TCTO and is documented on the AFTO Form 82
3 TYPES OF TCTOS
There are seven types TCTCT Modification, Inspection, Commodity, Companion, Supplement, Record, and Safety
The following paragraphs provide further descriptions of each of the seven TCTO types:
TCTOs may be accomplished at any level of maintenance: Organizational, Intermediate, or Depot
The PM in coordination with the Lead/Using MAJCOM determines the TCTO level of accomplishment
1 Modification TCTOs
These are often referred to as Configuration TCTOs
2 Inspection TCTOs
1 Special tools, parts and materials will be provided in TCTO kits when items are not commonly available at base level
There are three levels of priority for the seven types TCTOs:: Immediate Action, Urgent Action, and Routine Action
Immediate Action TCTOs are only issued with approval of the Lead MAJCOM to prevent use of equipment or pro- cedures until hazardous safety conditions, which pose an unacceptable risk of fatality or serious injury to personnel or extensive damage to or destruction of valuable property, can be corrected
1 The urgency of Immediate Action TCTOs requires rapid (immediate) action to remove the aircraft from service, prevent launch of missiles, discontinue operation of ground Communication-Elecrrniii (C-E) systems, or use of related support equipment, personal equipment or munitions
2 If O/I level capability for TCTO accomplishment is exceeded, resulting in a backlog, a request for maintenance assistance IAW TOs 00-25-107 or 00-25-108 may be submitted by the affected MAJCOM/Fund Holder
Contractors using or maintaining AF equipment shall accomplish all Immediate, Urgent, and Routine Action safety TCTOs and those non-safety TCTOs which are determined to be mission essential by the Owning Command
In identifying mission essential TCTOs, care should be exercised to select those TCTOs which could impair subsequent logistics support if not accomplished
TCTOs compliance shall be accomplished on deployed aircraff/cquipment within the specified compliance period
If necessary, only Immediate or Urgent Action TCTOs will be accomplished on transient aircraft (see TO 00-20-1)
2 It is USAF policy to offer AF system or commodity TCTOs approved for release to SAP customers
TCTOs must be reviewed for re- leasability by the local FDO
Compliance with the TCTOs will be IAW TO 00-5-19 and AFMAN 16-101

context_relevancy.query: List the three preservation levels that dictate protection against corrosion and environmental damage as per the USAF technical manual.
context_chunks:
This chapter is intended for use by depot and field level maintenance personnel as a guide for proper corrosion prevention and control of Ground Communications, Electronics, and Meteorological (C-E-M) equipment during storage and shipment
Packaging is used to prevent physical/mechanical damage or harm to items during storage, issue, or transfer and to provid protection from corrosion causing elements
) placed in storage or shipped to another activity (including depot) should be preserved in order to prevent deterioration from corrosion, mildew, mold, and decay as well as to protect from attack by micro organisms, vermin, or rodents
The type of preservation required for stored and/or shipped equipment and parts depends on the packaging pro- cess, material, containers and condition of the items before being packaged
Military Specifications, Standards, and Manuals
All activities involved with corrosion prevention and control of C-E-M equipmen during storage and shipment shall use this technical manual in conjunction with Technical Order (TO) 1-1-8, TO 1-1-689-series, TO 1-1-691, AFPAM (I) 24-237 and equipment system specific maintenance manuals as a guide for corrosion prevention and control
Numerous mili- tary specifications, standards, and manuals are mentioned throughout this chapter and should be referred to as directed
2 LEVELS OF PROTECTION
Specifying the level of protection provides the level of military preservation and packing that a given item requires to ensure that it is not de- graded during shipment and storage
Specific levels of protection are as follows:
1 Military Level of Preservation
This is the required preservation designed to protect an item during shipment, handling, indeterminate storage, and distribution to consignees worldwide
Preservatives should be applied whenever items require protection against deterioration and corrosion
Some preservatives protect items by providing a barrier against moisture, air, and other agents of corrosion and are known a contact preservatives
3 Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors
9 CLASSIFICATION OF PRESERVATIVES
There are preservatives for metals and for non-metals
Preservatives are classified on the basis of the material to be preserved and are either per manent or temporary
1 Preservation Methods
Preservation methods are standardized and have become generally known as Method 10 (physical protec- tion), Method 20 (preservative coating only), Method 30 (waterproof protection), Method 40 (water vapor proof protection) and Method 50 (water vapor proof protection with desiccant)
When there are no specific instructions, or for complete details on methods of preservation, general requirements, and suggested methods of application, refer to MIL-STD-2073-1 for a listing of the groups and their usage
The material in this manual provides basic cleaning, cоrro- sion prevention and control, and corrective maintenance in- formation to be used at organizational, intermediate, and de- pot levels
This manual is divided into eight chapters, two appendices, a glossary, and an alphabetical index
Chapter explains the appropriate usage of this manual
Chapter 3 outlines ac- cepted procedures, methods, and materials to be used in maintenance cleaning in Section I, lubrication in Section II, and preservation in Section III of aircraft, missiles, and re- lated equipment
Chapter 4 describes inspection techniques for detecting corrosion in Section and discusses corrosion prone areas in Section II
Chapter 5 outlines the approved methods for the removal of corrosion damage in Section I and the application of surface treatments in Section II
Chap- ter 6 covers recommended materials and procedures for the application of sealing compounds to aircraft, missile, and related equipment structures
Chapter g outlines emergency proce- dures to be followed after exposure of aircraft to salt water, fire extinguishing chemicals, etc
, Appendix A lists the rec- ommended materials for cleaning, corrosion prevention, sur- face treatment, and preservation of aircraft, missiles, and re- lated equipment
Appendix B lists equipment used for cleaning, corrosion removal, conversion coating, and sealing of aircraft, as well as associated safety equipment including personal protective equipment (PPE) for these operations
3 MIL-PRF-16173, Corrosion Preventive Com- pound, Solvent Cutback, Cold Application
MIL-PRF- 16173 covers five different grades of CPC's which can be applied by brushing or dipping
Grades 1, 2, and 4 do not displace water and must be applied to dried surfaces or to surfaces which have been treated with MIL-PRF-81309, Type II or III, MIL-L-87177, Grade B, or MIL-PRF-16173, Grade 3
Preservation of Specific Areas and Components
Areas requiring preservation should be noted and preserved on a scheduled basis
1 The following items may require preservation on a regular basis:
3 Preservative Materials
Table 8-8 contains a list of autho- rized preservative compounds for electronic equipment
Maintenance personnel should refer to the applicable equipment system specific technical manual for lubrication requirements

context_relevancy.query: What objects are prohibited inside Fuel Servicing Safety Zones?
context_chunks:
Spark producing devices are not authorized in the Fuels Servicing Safety Zone (FSSZ)
Clothing containing more than 65 percent of any combination or mixture of nylon, rayon, wool, or polyester will not be worn as outer garments in the FSSZ when fuel servicing aircraft with low flashpoint fuels (flashpoint 100 degrees Fahrenheit (ºF) or below)
Do not put on or remove outer garments while in the FSSZ
6 FUEL SERVICING SAFETY ZONE (FSSZ)
This is the area within 50 feet of a pressurized fuel carrying servicing component; i
The FSSZ applies to open ramps and to the insides of hangars and shelters that have been approved for fuel servicing operations
(Aircraft interiors are not considered part of the FSSZ unless canopies, ramps, or doors are open exposing part of the aircraft interior to a spilled or sprayed fuel hazard
) The fuel servicing safety zone is established and maintained during pressurization and move- ment of fuel
See Figure 3-1 for an example of the bomber pit refueling safety zone and Figure 3-2 for an example of the fighter refueling safety zone
During fuel movement, active ignition sources shall be removed and kept out of the fuel servic- ing safety zone
Non-servicing vehicles and AGE Equipment (with engines not running) are allowed to be parked within the Fuel Servicing Safety Zone as long as they do not prevent the emergency egress of the servicing equipment or personnel
An aircraft adjacent to a fuel servicing safety zone can have its engines running as long as the aircraft thrust is not directed at the aircraft being refueled
Non-essential aircraft electrical systems, including radar, shall not be activated on the aircraft during servicing operations un less absolutely required for servicing
Passengers may enter or exit the aircraft during fuel servicing provided either: a jetway is used; or, if a mobile staircase or aircraft integral stairs are used and the fuel servicing operation is on the opposite side of the aircraft and the passengers do not come closer than 25 feet from any aircraft fuel vent outlet
The 40K loader open-flame heater will not be used within the Fuel Servicing Safety Zone (FSSZ)
PRIOR TO CONCURRENT FUEL SERVICING OPERATIONS hhe CSS must:
Verify that any civilian vehicles, such as catering trucks
Aircraft parked as prescribed in AFMAN 32-1084 and which intrude into the fuel servicing safety zone shall not be:
(2) Disconnecting combustible fluid carrying lines, except those equipped with non-spill, quick disconnects
9 SERVICING CONSTRAINTS
The following constraints apply during servicing operations:
During servicing, only those personnel actually required for the operation shall remain in the fuel servicing safety zone
Personnel receiving training on specific tasks required to complete the fuel servicing operation are allowed in the FSSZ if they are under the direct control and supervision of the fuel servicing supervisor
There shall be no smoking within 50 feet of any aircraft or servicing operation
Servicing operations shall not begin if any indication of "hot brakes" are noted
Fuel servicing team will consist of a minimum of seven personnel
Do not touch the skin of the aircraft or other metal objects, including any portion of fuel servicing vehicle(s)
Headgear will not be worn in the immediate area of an operating engine
Maintenance during fuel servicing is limited to physical replace- ment of avionics components inside the aircraft
Number three engine will not be operated during hot refueling

context_relevancy.query: What color represents hazardous elements under the USAF's color-coding system to mitigate physical risks?
context_chunks:
Safety color coding of new and old machines in accordance with the steps below will clarify OSHA Standard 20 CFR 1910
1 Color coding Machines and Machine Guards, AFMAN91-203, Color Coding and other safety agencies
These protective color codes are mandatory by Federal OSHA
(1) Use of color is visual signal that communicates important information quickly
Defined safety color codes: red for danger or stop, yellow for caution, orange for warning, green for safety, blue for information, magenta or purple for radiation, and black with white or yellow for directions or bounders
(a) Safety red will be the basic color for identifying stop, off, or hot
Stop buttons or electrical switches used for emergency stopping of machinery shall be safety red only
Items that get 140 Degrees Fahrenheit (ºF) or greater shall be painted safety red
(b) Safety yellow will be used as the basic color for designating physical hazards such as strike against, stumbling, falling, tripping and/or caught in between
Yellow will be the basic color for designating other cautions and can be used for hand levers, cranks, knobs, locks, and access doors
Marking of lines requiring metal tags does not normally apply to airborne systems
Tapes or decals, as applicable, will be used for airborne lines, except that small tags of some lightweight noncorrosive metal such as aluminum alloy, stamped with contents, pressure, and direction of flow, may be used on airborne lines where temperature extreme or oily environment makes use of tapes and decals infeasible
Function shall be identified by the use of words, colors, and symbols, except that yymblls need not to be used where identification is accomplished with paints and tags, and neither colors nor symbols are equired on aluminum alloy tags
The primary hazard associated with the contents of any line shall be identified in black etters on a white background
The words and abbreviations in Table 1-47 are to be used to identify specific classes of hazards
A selected list of hazards associated with common line contents are shown in Table 1-48
TO 1Hazard warning or safety precautions which will prevent injury to personnel or damage to equipment
The fire access panel or doors in the engine compart- ments shall have contrasting color boarder and identifying text
14 Marking of Emergency Lighting (Flashlight) for Cargo and Transport Aircraft
Aircraft which have flashlights adjacent to natural or emergency exits will have 1/2 inch yellow band around the base of the flashlight mount
Composite or honeycomb panels on upper surfaces shall be dis- tinctly marked by contrasting color I-1/2 inch wide hash marks

context_relevancy.query: Are the HF radios/radar prohibited within 300 feet unless technical orders specify exceptions (e.g., SATCOM antennas angled 10°+ above the horizon in the USAF technical manuals?
context_chunks:
Aircraft radar and High Frequency (HF) radios shall not be operated in the transmit mode within 300 feet of servicing operations, unless otherwise specified in the applicable aircraft and equipment repair technical orders, or when TO 312- 10-4 procedures are used to ensure a safe distance
Satellite communications (SATCOM) radios may be operated in the transmit mode if the antenna beam is pointed at least ten degrees above the horizon
Do not start servicing operations (any movement of fuel, water, environmental fluid, oil, hydraulic fluid, oxygen, nitro- gen, or hydrazine) whenever one of the following conditions exists
(1) When a lightning advisory has been issued indicating an electrical storm is within five nautical miles (5
(2) Winds reach velocities hazardous to the aircraft or servicing operations
Specific high wind restrictions will be de- veloped by base-level personnel for each base supporting a flying mission
All unattended vehicles on the flight line will be parked so they will not interfere with the aircraft being towed or taxied
Refueling/defuellng operations will not be performed:
(1) Within 300 feet of aircraft radar and High Frequency (HF) radios operating in the transmit mode unless otherwise speci- fied in applicable aircraft and equipment TOs, or when TO 31Z-10-4 procedures are used
(2) Within a 50 foot radius of operating aircraft, or with aircraft turned in such a way that engine exhaust is directed upon fueling/defueling operation
Transmitting on aircraft HF radios, or operating radar, radar altimeter, or SKE equipment will not be done during con- current servicing operations
Inertial Navigation System (INS) and Fuel Saving Advisory System (FSAS) may re- main energized
Satellite Communications (SATCOM) radios may be operated in the transmit mode if the antenna bean is pointed at least ten degrees above the horizon
Power-on maintenance of electrical equipment on the exterior of the aircraft is prohibited, unless the equipment is lo- cated outside the FSSZ
Maintenance or repair of the aircraft or engine fuel systems which require the opening of fuel lines, fuel tanks, or re- placement of plug-in components is prohibited
Make sure vent mast is not obstructed
Make sure refueling is not done within 300 feet of ground radar equipment
Make sure refueling is not done within 50 feet of other aircraft with engine(s) operating
Operation of the Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) is prohibited during normal refueling
Fuel pressure from servicing equipment should not exceed 55 PSI, during normal refueling
Make sure aircraft is receiving correct fuel (JP-4, JP-5 or JP-8)
Separation distances between fuel systems repair facilities/areas and aircraft radar should be provided in the specific weapons system TOs
Ifa TO is not available, or it does not contain separation distances, 300 feet is the required minimum distance (100 feet when using a dummy load)
2 Non-intrinsically Safe Radios and Cellular Telephones
Non-intrinsically safe radios and all cellular telephones shall remain outside of the open fuel system repair area
6 for the use of non-intrinsically safe radios and telephones
3 Intrinsically-safe Intercoms, Radios, Mobile Radios and Telephones
Intrinsically-safe radios, mobile radios/ intercoms, or telephones listed for National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 70, Class I, Division 1, hazardous areas, may be used
Intercoms, radios and telephones are permitted in fuel systems repair facilities/areas to maintain communications between members of the confined space entry team and other personnel
Restrictions оfTO 31Z-10-4 shall apply

STORAGE (STATUS OF PRESERVATION AND PROCESSING) INSPECTIONS FOR VEHICLES PRESERVED FOR
1 Visual Inspection
Vehicles and their attachments and OVE shall be given a visual inspection each 90 day storage period or more frequently contingent upon climatic conditions and determination by the VFM
This inspection shall be visual and normally requires no removal of preservation or component disassembly beyond removal of cover and access plates, hatches, etc
This inspection shall include the following:
Where quantity of vehicles in storage justifies, the VFM may perform this check on a sampling basis
2 During Storage
Operators shall report defects detected during exercise to storage facility inspectors to ensure proper condition tagging of units found other than serviceable
As the vehicles are maintained in serviceable status and immediate availability for delivery is mandatory, shipping inspection requirements shall be held to a minimum
The following inspections are required:
Ensure that vehicle is complete and serviced, that OVE, records, publications, batteries, etc
Check to ensure that the shipment complies with applicable traffic and carrier tariff regulations
2 Vehicles in Use
Determination of corrosion center to be used: Request for routine shipment instructions should also contain a request for the vehicle to be processed through a corrosion treatment facility
Level A the requirements specified for this level are intended to provide adequate protection to vehicles and components fron corrosion, deterioration, and physical damage during shipment, handling and varying periods of storage in excess of 90 days from date of preservation
Vehicles with bio-diesel fuel will have their tank drained, filters changed, and standard diesel added prior to being preserved to Level A
Level B the requirements specified for this level are intended to provide adequate protection for domestic or oversea shipmen (open deck loading excluded) and which may involve outside storage for a combined total of approximately 90 days
Level C the requirements specified for this level are intended to provide adequate protection during domestic shipments to immediate use locations and for vehicles Non-Mission Capable Supply (NMCS) for less than 90 days
Live Storage and Air Shipments

The clean room officer will be responsible to ensure that clean room garments are clean, and meet limited linting requirements after washing
Garments required to be worn in en- vironmentally controlled facilities shall be functional and job oriented
They should be made of woven polyester fabrics and designed to minimize contamination from the garment
All apparel shall be designed with a minimum of seams, no pockets, pleats, raw edges, or dust collection features
General type garments are coveralls, smocks, frocks, hoods, snoods, caps, shoe covers, boots, gloves, and wiping cloths
A sample of all new garments purchased shall be submitted to the clean room officer for tests to ensure that requirements are met before acceptance
All clean room garments and accessories shall be her- metically sealed in clean polyethylene bags having two mil minimum thickness
Each garment will be packaged individually except shoe covers may be packaged in pairs
Each apparel package shall be marked as to size S- M-L, or packed such that garment size marking is clearly visible
Wiping cloths shall be packaged in multiples of 10 per package
Based upon the laundry procedures described in Paragraph 8
A garment ex- hibits proper cleanliness and limited linting properties if it has less than 2,000 particles per square foot 5
Garments will be monitored using one of the procedures described in Chapter 9
All cleaning, washing, drying, and packaging shall be accomplished with the clean environment
Steps may be taken to eliminate static electricity on garments if static is a problem as determined on a lo- cal basis
2 Garment Changes
After laundering, a final inspection will be individually made of garments for damage, loose fibers, or lint bearing seams
These garments will be separated for repair or disposal
If a garment becomes soiled at any time while per- forming clean room duties, it shall be changed imme- diately (determined by clean room management)
5 Packaging of Garments
After cleaning, the laun- dry shall package and deliver all clean room garments in numbered lots which can be traced to known wash load
The cleaning procedure will prevent the cleaned shoe from becoming re-contami- nated upon removal from the unit
, have been moved from shoes and clothes
When donning shoes or shoe covers, personnel will not place the clean room shoe or shoe cover in the area previously occupied by the street shoe

5 If small shoe covers or clean room shoes are used, hen the coverall pants leg shall fit snugly

context_relevancy.query: What are the proper greasing instructions for ratchet lever jacks?
context_chunks:
Periodically check hydraulic fluid lever in push-pull hydraulic jacks
Ratchet lever jacks should be well greased
The bushings should be filled with grease in the small hole provided for greasing purposes
2 Ratchet Lever Jacks
A vertical ratchet lever jack has a rack bar that is raised or lowered through a ratchet lever
An outrigger ratche jack is ratchet operated and has an extra reverse ratchet handle and a base plate
The jack will raise the load (3) with every degree turned on the handle
Screw the jack all the way down in the lower housing and withdraw the handle or bar for storage upon completion of the job
USING A RATCHET LEVER JACK
To lower a load, push reversing lever to straight-down position and operate the lever
The jack will lower the load one notch for each upstroke of the lever
The reversing lever (1) is located below the lifting mechanism cover (2) on the left side of jack when viewed from the lifting lever
To raise a load, pull reversing lever up and toward the operator and operate the lever (4)
The jack will raise the load on notch for each lever downstroke

context_relevancy.query: What are the three parts of a reamer as per the USAF technical manuals and orders?
context_chunks:
AIR FORCE AIR FORCE TO 32-1-101 ARMY ARMY TM 9-243 NAVY (NAVAIR) NAVY M6290-AJ-MAN-1010 MARINE CORPS MARINE CORP TM 10209-10/1
Reamers are used to enlarge and true a hole
The reamer consists of three parts, the body (1), the shank (2), and the blades (3)
The shank has a square tang to allow the reamer to be held with a wrench for turning
1 Solid Straight-Hole Reamer
A solid straight-hole reamer is made of one solid piece of high-speed steel having a straight shank and straight (1) or spiral flutes (2)
Reamers are also available in sets containing 25 reamers in 1/64 inch increments from 1/8 inch to 1/2 inch
The sets may be mixed to include straight and taper pin reamers
Each reamer size is stamped on the shank of the tool
Solid straight-hole reamers are used for most work since they are the most accurate and the most rugged of the straight-hole reamers
2 Solid Taper-Pin Reamer
Solid taper-pin reamers are used to finish tapered holes for the insertion of tapered pins or other tapered parts
Solid taper-pin reamers come with straight (1) or spiral flutes (2)
They are also included in mixed sets of straight and taper-pin reamers
3 Expansion Reamer
Expansion reamers are adjustable, and their sizes may be changed by 1/8 inch for a 1 inch reamer and 5/16 inch for a 2 inc reamer
AIR FORCE AIR FORCE TO 32-1-101 ARMY ARMY TM 9-243 NAVY (NAVAIR) NAVY M6290-AJ-MAN-1010 MARINE CORPS MARINE CORP TM 10209-10/1
The diameter of the reamer is changed by screwing in or backing out the screw plug The standard sizes range from 1/4 inch to I inch, by 32nds
A 1/4 inch expansion reamer will enlarge the hole to a 9/32 inch hole, etc
4 Adjustable-Blade Reamer
The blades of an adjustable reamer are separate from the body and are fitted into grooves in the threaded shank of the tool
Adjusting nuts located below and above the blades control the diameter of the reamer
The reamers come with straight (1) or spiral flutes (2), with or without a floating pilot on solid mandrels, and in several sizes
Adjustable reamers are also available sets
Pipe reamers are made of carbon steel
Most pipe reamers are designed to receive a T-handle (1)
3 USING A SOLID STRAIGHT-HOLE REAMER
AIR FORCE AIR FORCE TO 32-1-101 ARMY ARMY TM 9-243 NAVY (NAVAIR) NAVY M6290-AJ-MAN-1010 MARINE CORPS MARINE CORP TM 10209-10/1
Tube Cutting And Flaring Tools
Air Force Technical Order
AIR FORCE AIR FORCE TO 32-1-101 ARMY ARMY TM 9-243 NAVY (NAVAIR) NAVY M6290-AJ-MAN-1010 MARINE CORPS MARINE CORP TM 10209-10/1
If the proper pressure is applied in use and the reamer chatters, replace it to insure accurate work
To prevent chipping or dulling the reamer when you are reaming a hole, turn the reamer in the cutting direction only
To prevent damage to reamers for long and short term storage, ensure reamers are cleaned of all debris and individually separated where they will not come in contact with each other, or other tools
If at all possible, store the reamers in their original shipping containers
Generally most of the different type reamers may be used for aluminum, but for best results the spiral fluted reamers are recommended -olld, expansion or adjustable
The following procedures and tools are recommended for reaming aluminum alloys:

context_relevancy.query: What are the Safety and equipment protocols of Military Cargo Aircraft, and how do they verify GPU/APU positioning?
context_chunks:
Ensure aircraft is bonded to refueling equipment
Ground Power Unit (GPU) must be positioned 50 feet minimum, and to the maximum extent of the power cable, preferably upwind of fuel servicing area, and within 45 degrees either side of aircraft nose
If a GPU is not available and if carrier's manual so authorizes, aircraft Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) may be used dur- ing refueling
If APU is used during refueling, fuel truck must be positioned 50 feet from the APU exhaust out- let
(3) Ensure GPU is positioned and connected when re- quired
(4) Ensure required maintenance stands are correctly po- sitioned and bonded to aircraft
(5) Ensure aircraft stairs/ladders are correctly positioned and unobstructed to enable emergency egress
(2) Chock aircraft
(a) Requirement for a Ground Power Unit (GPU)
(c) Status/condition of aircraft and its systems
Ground Power Unit (GPU) must be positioned outside the fuel servicing zone and when possible, uphill of the fuel ser- vicing area, pressurized fuel servicing equipment, and fuel vents
Failure to comply could result in damage to, or destruc- tion of, equipment or loss of mission effectiveness
(b) *Positioning and connecting GPU
Wet wing defueling shall be conducted in accordance with published aircraft and fuels equipment technical orders/ checklists
For C-5 aircraft, wet wing defueling is accomplished using electrical power from either a USAF-supplied GPU or the aircraft APU
For C-130 aircraft, electrical power is supplied from either a GPU or by running number I and 2 engines and number 3 and 4 engines in low ground idle or in hotel mode
For KC-135E aircraft (after TCTO 1388, 180L APU), electrical power can be supplied from either a GPU, by running an outboard engine, or the 180L APU
Refer to Table 6-4 for aircraft approved for wet wing defueling
either a GPU, by running an outboard engine, or the 180L APU
inspections, or operating permitted aircraft systems; C-130 may do so with engines running
Aircraft electrical power may be provided by either an external Ground Power Unit (GPU) or by operating aircraft engine number or 2 and/or the aircraft APU

If a GPU is not available and if carrier's manual so authorizes, aircraft Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) may be used dur- ing refueling
If APU is used during refueling, fuel truck must be positioned 50 feet from the APU exhaust out- let
(3) Ensure GPU is positioned and connected when re- quired
(4) Ensure required maintenance stands are correctly po- sitioned and bonded to aircraft
(5) Ensure aircraft stairs/ladders are correctly positioned and unobstructed to enable emergency egress
(2) Chock aircraft
(a) Requirement for a Ground Power Unit (GPU)
(c) Status/condition of aircraft and its systems
Ground Power Unit (GPU) must be positioned outside the fuel servicing zone and when possible, uphill of the fuel ser- vicing area, pressurized fuel servicing equipment, and fuel vents
Failure to comply could result in damage to, or destruc- tion of, equipment or loss of mission effectiveness
(b) *Positioning and connecting GPU
Wet wing defueling shall be conducted in accordance with published aircraft and fuels equipment technical orders/ checklists
For C-5 aircraft, wet wing defueling is accomplished using electrical power from either a USAF-supplied GPU or the aircraft APU
For C-130 aircraft, electrical power is supplied from either a GPU or by running number I and 2 engines and number 3 and 4 engines in low ground idle or in hotel mode
For KC-135E aircraft (after TCTO 1388, 180L APU), electrical power can be supplied from either a GPU, by running an outboard engine, or the 180L APU

either a GPU, by running an outboard engine, or the 180L APU
inspections, or operating permitted aircraft systems; C-130 may do so with engines running

context_relevancy.query: Operations that Mattocks are designed for.
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 43 MATTOCKS
1 HOW TO CHOOSE AND USE MATTOCKS
2, provides you with a list of some of the types of mattocks
These pages should help you select the right mattock to do the job
Using the Mattock, Paragraph 43
4 tells you how to use the mattock to perform the desired function
Care of Mattocks, Paragraph 43
2 MATTOCKS TYPES AND USES
The mattock is designed for digging and cutting operations
The mattock can have a single or double-beveled head
However, the single beveled mattock can be combined with other digging tools to perform a variety of functions
For example, the "pick-mattock" is a combination of the single-beveled mattock and pick
3 MATTOCKS SAFETY
When using a mattock, it is important to have a firm footing and correct posture to prevent the mattock from glancing and striking the feet or legs if the mark is missed
When practicing using the mattock, swing with either the right or the left band leading
When your position becomes tiring, reverse your hands on the handle of the mattock
The knees should be set but not tense
The feet should be spread apart at a comfortable distance
When practicing using the mattock, swing with either the right or the left band leading
When your position becomes tiring, reverse your hands on the handle of the mattock
Raise the mattock over the right shoulder
Swing the mattock down toward the work, allowing the right hand to slide back along the handle toward the left hand so that at the finish of the swing, the hands are close together
With the left hand in the center of the handle, the mattock is swung in the same manner, except that the positions are reversed
Light swings are accomplished with wrist motion only, allowing the head of the mattock to do the work
Slight prying may be done with the mattock
5 CARE OF MATTOCKS
Clean the mattock thoroughly after use and before short or long-term storage
Store the mattock so that the head will not be struck against metal or other hard surfaces
The mattock should be placed on its head against a wall or hung on a rack for storage
The raw wood handles of mattocks should be rubbed thoroughly (preferably prior to use) with a cloth soaked in boiled linseed oil
This will prevent drying, checking, and moisture penetration

3 | USING THE GASKET CUTTER | 44-3 |

context_relevancy.query: Explain the functions of Corrosion Prevention Compounds and identify the five types of authorized cleaning compounds of MIL-PRF-85570.
context_chunks:
A listing of the most current QPL/QPD for a specific cleaning compound can be found on the Defense Logistics Agency website for specifications, handbooks, and QPL/QPD data http://s/ssstt
miiiiieo on the Air Force Corrosion Prevention and Control office website located on the Air Force Portal
2 Authorized cleaning materials, mixing ratios and cleaning procedures that may be used on SE are shown in Table MIL-PRF-87937, Cleaning Compound Aerospace Equipment, is the preferred cleaning compound for SE
However, if MIL-PRF-87937 is not available, MIL-PRF-85570; Cleaning Compound Aircraft Exterior, may be used
The following is prief general description of the different cleaning compounds
1 MIL-PRF-85570, Cleaning Compound Aircraft Exterior, Type I, is for general use on exterior surfaces, and is fffctive for use on heavy soils and grease
For information on and most current revision of the many cleaning and corrosion prevention and control material and process related specifications as well as the most current QPLs for those specifications having a QPL are available from the ASSIST Quick Search, website http://asiiidda
1 Cleaning Compounds
Cleaning compounds work by dissolving soluble soils, emulsifying oily soils, and suspending solid soils
There are several types of cleaning compounds, each of which cleans a surface using one or more of these mechanisms
MIL-PRF-87937, CLEANING COMPOUND, AIRCRAFT
MIL-PRF-85570, CLEANING COMPOUND, AIRCRAFT
1 Cleaning compounds conforming to MIL-PRF-87937 and MIL-PRF-85570 and that are listed on the QPL for each specification have been tested and proven compatible with polyamide insulation
Types I and IV of MIL-PRF-87937 and Types I and II of MIL-PRF- 85570 all contain detergents and foaming agents and work the same way as any detergent solution
Ma terials conforming to and listed on the QPL/QPD for MIL PRF-29602, Type (liquid concentrate) and Type II (pow der) Cleaning Compounds for Parts Washers and Spra: Cabinets, either diluted with water (Type I) or dissolved ii water (Type II) in accordance with manufacturer's instruc tions are the cleaning agents to be used in high pressur cabinet style parts washers for removing oils and grease from disassembled components
5, MIL-PRF 29602 cleaning solutions can attack/corrode aluminum al loys
Solvents used in MIL-PRF-85570, Type IV clean- ers may be hazardous air pollutants (HAP's)
MIL-PRF-85570, Type III is a cleaner that contains detergents and fine abrasive particles and is intended for removal of exhaust gas and gun blast residues from aircraft with high gloss coating system
4 CLEANING COMPOUNDS
Cleaning compounds work by dissolving soluble soils, emulsifying oily soils, and suspending solid soils
There are several types of cleaning compounds, each of which cleans a surface using one or more of these mechanisms
MIL-PRF-87937, CLEANING COMPOUND, AIRCRAFT
MIL-PRF-85570, CLEANING COMPOUND, AIRCRAFT
Cleaning compounds conforming to MIL-PRF-87937 and MIL-PRF-85570 and that are listed on the Qualified Products List (QPL) for each specification have been tested and proven compatible with polyimide insulation
CPC or preservatives are used to protect metal equipment parts and components by preventing corrosive materials from contacting and corrod- ing bare metal surfaces
Many of these compounds are also able to displace water, including sea water, and other contaminants from these surfaces, and some provide lubrication, as well as corrosion protection

context_relevancy.query: What is the proper cleaning protocol for Airforce hoses after spray painting?
context_chunks:
A spray gun contains parts that must work together
Dismantling a spray gun after each paint operation will cause excessive damage to the gun and its seals
A spray gun should only be dismantled when a proper spray pattern cannot be produced
When lower vapor pressure solvents are used to clean paint guns and their paint supply lines/hoses, the paint guns must be blown out with clean, compressed air and the lines/hoses must be flushed with MIL-DTL-81772, Type I thinner immediately after they are removed from the paint gun washer and prior to use to eliminate all traces of these solvents from the guns and lines/hoses
These solvents are not compatible with the paints and primers used on AF equipment, and failure to remove them will contaminate the next paint system applied with the paint gun and supply lines/hoses and cause fish eyes and other paint curing problems
The most effective method for cleaning paint spray guns, paint fluid hoses, and paint pots/cups is by using a mechanical paint gun washer
The container has devices installed inside of it for connecting paint guns for cleaning of internal as well as external surfaces
Figure 4-6 illustrates proper installation of an air supply for paint spraying
Hoses shall always be thoroughly cleaned by flushing with solvent appropriate for the coating material used
When the material is a catalyzed (two-component) system (epoxy or polyurethane), this must be done immediately after use to prevent the material from curing in the hose
Do not use hoses longer than 50 feet
The high-pressure air hose shall have a minimum Inside Diameter (ID) of 7/16 inch
The fluid hose from the tank to the gun shall be no less than 3/8 inch ID and the air hose from the regulator to the tank and from the tank to the gun shall be no less than 5/16 inch ID
Air and paint hoses are furnished in various standard lengths
CHAPTER 54 PAINT APPLICATION
To insure that all painting accomplished at the sites meets high quality workmanship standards, the proper method of paint application (both brush and spray) will be explained in this chapter
The method and type of metal being painted and its condition
Most touch up work will be done by spray painting
The procedure to use when brush painting is as follows:
Clean the metal to be painted, using approved cleaning agents
Apply one coat of grey primer sealer (PN 3034742) and allow it to dry
All safety and regulating features on associated spray painting equipment and safety equipment shall be operational
All aircraft, equipment, work stands, solvents containers/buckets, people, and adjacent equipment shall be grounded prior to using the painting equipment
Prior to cleaning electrostatic paint guns, the operators shall ensure that they and the gun are grounded and that the equipment is deenergized
All spray nozzles and auxiliary equipment being cleaned with flammable solvents shall be done inside the paint hangar facility with the ventilation system operating
CHAPTER 4 PAINTING APPLICATION METHODS
Spray application is the standard for painting Air Force (AF) aircraft and most other equipment
2 SPRAY METHODS
The AF uses several hand-operated spray methods: hot spray, cold and/or hot airless, air assisted airless, HVLP spray, and any of these methods in conjunction with electrostatics
The HVLP method is now the standard spray method used in the AF and meets the requirements of environmental regulations
1 HVLP Spray
In this method, the coating material is atomized by a high volume of low pressure air through the spray gun nozzle
Low pressure air between I and 10 PSI is used to atomize the coating material at the spray head

context_relevancy.query: What is the required distance of pressurized fuel components from ignition sources as defined by Fuel Servicing Safety Zones as per the USAF technical manuals?
context_chunks:
6 FUEL SERVICING SAFETY ZONE (FSSZ)
This is the area within 50 feet of a pressurized fuel carrying servicing component; i
, and 25 feet around aircraft fuel vent outlets
The FSSZ applies to open ramps and to the insides of hangars and shelters that have been approved for fuel servicing operations
(Aircraft interiors are not considered part of the FSSZ unless canopies, ramps, or doors are open exposing part of the aircraft interior to a spilled or sprayed fuel hazard
) The fuel servicing safety zone is established and maintained during pressurization and move- ment of fuel
See Figure 3-1 for an example of the bomber pit refueling safety zone and Figure 3-2 for an example of the fighter refueling safety zone
During fuel movement, active ignition sources shall be removed and kept out of the fuel servic- ing safety zone
Non-servicing vehicles and AGE Equipment (with engines not running) are allowed to be parked within the Fuel Servicing Safety Zone as long as they do not prevent the emergency egress of the servicing equipment or personnel
An aircraft adjacent to a fuel servicing safety zone can have its engines running as long as the aircraft thrust is not directed at the aircraft being refueled
Operating external power units will be parked at least 50 feet from pressurized fuel carrying servicing components and at least 25 feet from aircraft fuel vents during fuel servicing operations
They will be placed outside the fuel servicing safety zone and when possible positioned upwind from the servicing operation
Vehicles authorized inside the fuel servicing safety zone during concurrent servicing operations shall maintain at least a 25-foot separation distance from the aircraft fuel vent outlets (except Boeing 747/757/767 aircraft, including KC-46, which are acceptable for having authorized vehicles pass underneath but may not stop or be parked directly beneath the fuel vent outlets) and a 25-foot separation distance from pressurized fuel servicing system components during fuel servicing operations
Fuel Servicing Safety Zone (Defined as an area extending 50 feet from pressurized fuel servicing components and 25 feet from aircraft fuel vent outlets
| CFR | Crash/Fire/Rescue Vehicle |
| FSSZ | Fuel Servicing Safety Zone (Defined as an |
The F-22 can be refueled with brake temperatures exceeding 750 oF if an adequate shield is used to keep vented fuel from reaching the left main landing gear brake assembly
If glowing or crackling fuel is noted when servicing aircraft, immediately cease all servicing operations
Operating external power units will be parked outside the fuel servicing safety zone which is at least 50 feet from the pressurized fuel carrying servicing components and at least 25 feet from aircraft fuel vent outlets
The operating units will be positioned upwind from the fuel servicing operation when possible
Maintenance and repair of aircraft electrical, radio, radar, fuel, or other systems requiring use of electrical power, shall not be accomplished during fueling operations
Vehicles shall not be allowed to operate/pass within 25 feet of aircraft fuel vents and pressurized fuel servicing sys- tem components (except Boeing 747/757/777 aircraft, in- cluding KC-46, which are acceptable for having authorized vehicles pass underneath but may not stop or be parked directly beneath the fuel vent outlets)
A malfunction of any component of the fueling system will require an immediate shutdown of the fueling operation until defect is repaired
All ground power units and air conditioners will be con- nected prior to starting fuel servicing
Equipment shall re- main connected until fuel servicing is terminated
High-lift trucks will not be raised/lowered or moved within the FSSZ while refueling equipment is pressurized unless the high-lift truck is designed, approved and maintained for use within, a hazardous location

(Aircraft interiors are not considered part of the FSSZ unless canopies, ramps, or doors are open exposing part of the aircraft interior to a spilled or sprayed fuel hazard

See Figure 3-1 for an example of the bomber pit refueling safety zone and Figure 3-2 for an example of the fighter refueling safety zone
During fuel movement, active ignition sources shall be removed and kept out of the fuel servic- ing safety zone

Non-servicing vehicles and AGE Equipment (with engines not running) are allowed to be parked within the Fuel Servicing Safety Zone as long as they do not prevent the emergency egress of the servicing equipment or personnel
An aircraft adjacent to a fuel servicing safety zone can have its engines running as long as the aircraft thrust is not directed at the aircraft being refueled
Operating external power units will be parked at least 50 feet from pressurized fuel carrying servicing components and at least 25 feet from aircraft fuel vents during fuel servicing operations
They will be placed outside the fuel servicing safety zone and when possible positioned upwind from the servicing operation

Vehicles authorized inside the fuel servicing safety zone during concurrent servicing operations shall maintain at least a 25-foot separation distance from the aircraft fuel vent outlets (except Boeing 747/757/767 aircraft, including KC-46, which are acceptable for having authorized vehicles pass underneath but may not stop or be parked directly beneath the fuel vent outlets) and a 25-foot separation distance from pressurized fuel servicing system components during fuel servicing operations
Fuel Servicing Safety Zone (Defined as an area extending 50 feet from pressurized fuel servicing components and 25 feet from aircraft fuel vent outlets
| CFR | Crash/Fire/Rescue Vehicle |
| FSSZ | Fuel Servicing Safety Zone (Defined as an |
The F-22 can be refueled with brake temperatures exceeding 750 oF if an adequate shield is used to keep vented fuel from reaching the left main landing gear brake assembly
If glowing or crackling fuel is noted when servicing aircraft, immediately cease all servicing operations

Operating external power units will be parked outside the fuel servicing safety zone which is at least 50 feet from the pressurized fuel carrying servicing components and at least 25 feet from aircraft fuel vent outlets
The operating units will be positioned upwind from the fuel servicing operation when possible
Maintenance and repair of aircraft electrical, radio, radar, fuel, or other systems requiring use of electrical power, shall not be accomplished during fueling operations

Vehicles shall not be allowed to operate/pass within 25 feet of aircraft fuel vents and pressurized fuel servicing sys- tem components (except Boeing 747/757/777 aircraft, in- cluding KC-46, which are acceptable for having authorized vehicles pass underneath but may not stop or be parked directly beneath the fuel vent outlets)
A malfunction of any component of the fueling system will require an immediate shutdown of the fueling operation until defect is repaired
All ground power units and air conditioners will be con- nected prior to starting fuel servicing
Equipment shall re- main connected until fuel servicing is terminated

High-lift trucks will not be raised/lowered or moved within the FSSZ while refueling equipment is pressurized unless the high-lift truck is designed, approved and maintained for use within, a hazardous location

context_relevancy.query: Should paint be thinned excessively to increase the flow of atomized fluid?
context_chunks:
1 The first consideration in obtaining this balance is the proper combination of air cap and fluid tip for the particular material being sprayed as recommended by the equipment manufacturer and the coating material manufacturer
These adjustments are necessary to obtain proper atomization and other desired spraying characteristics required for the job and conditions
The fluid adjusting screw on the gun (Figure 4-10, B) permits restriction of fluid flow relative to the volume of air being used, but is limited since it puts additional tension on the gun trigger and tends to discourage feathering at the beginning and end of the stroke
3 For pressure feed spraying, adjustment of tank pressure and adjustment of the atomization air supply to the gun is more effective for obtaining the proper air to fluid balance
4 The air adjustment screw (Figure 4-10, A) can be changed to spread the atomized fluid out over a greater area, which, in combination with the increased air flow, is equivalent to reducing the flow of fluid
not thin the paint excessively to increase the flow of fluid
The correct atomizing pressure depends on the type of coating and the length and diameter of the air line from the regulator to the gun
The pressure must be sufficient to completely atomize the material being sprayed but no greater
For HVLP paint guns, the regulator pressure shall be adjusted to a lower level to ensure the nozzle pressure does not ex- ceed a maximum of 10 PSI in accordance with Paragraph 4
An excessive amount of air may give a split pattern in which the material deposited is light in the middle of the pattern; too little air may give a heavy centered pattern

context_relevancy.query: What height, in inches, do the letters and numbers used for vehicle components & warnings range from?
context_chunks:
8 Identification Markings of Jettisonable Aircraft Components
1 inch letters, 6 inches from the forward left-hand end, on ei- 8
All ejection seats shall have the radio call number stenciled on the seat back near the top edge
The numbers and letters shall be 1 inch high and positioned to avoid contact with parachute gear
The stencil will be I inch high and applied near the component center on both sides
9 Markings for Servicing, Ground Handling, and Hazard Warning
Lettering shall be in accordance with Appendix B of this TO
Symbols for the identification of service points, ground handling, and hazard warnings shall be applied to the required locations on all USAF aircraft
Size of lettering to be minimum o
5 inches to a maximum of 6 inches
3 Call Signs/Numbers
Vehicle call signs/numbers wi be affixed to each side of the vehicle and on the roof as prescribed by NFPA Standards 414
As space permits, the side numbers shall be a minimum of 16 inches
As space permits, top numbers shall be a minimum of 24 inches in height and affixed with their base facing towards the front of the vehicle
The color for call sign numbers shall be in sharp contrast to the vehicle color
The shield must fit within the "UNITED STATES" and "AIR FORCE" and not exceed 12 inches in height and width
Magnetic markings are not authorized for Fire vehicles
All snow removal equipment will be outlined to indicate height, length, and width using silver reflective tape conforming to ASTM D4956, Type II, Class 3, Type 1, color G
When warning lights and sirens are used, they will be installed on a removable mounting bar NSN 2540-00-409-8878
The abbreviated legend "E
" will be applied to a metal backing plate in 2 inch silver reflective letters on a red background and affixed to the front and rear of the mounting bar
The following vehicle registration numbers will be allowed to retain the large lettering on the sides of the body that reads "USAF BOD"
MARKING VEHICLES EQUIPPED WITH MS51335 AND COMMERCIAL ITEM DESCRIPTION (CID) A-A-52550 SERI
All vehicles (except toned down) equipped with a manual release pintle hook, MS51335 and CID A-A-52550 Series, will be marked as illustrated in Figure 2-19 using 1/2 inch silver or white reflective letters on a red background
and telephone line maintenanee/connsrutiionn vehicles that are not insulated and not designed for working in close proximity of electrical power lines shall be equipped with a metal sign, approximately 8 by 10 inches
On other applicable vehicles, sign shall be affixed to the operators ground control panel and basket control panel
This sign shall have white background with "DANGER" in 2 inch red letters; "DO NOT OPERATE BOOM OR DERRICK WITHIN 10 FEET OF ELECTRIC LINES" in 1/2 inch black letters
In addition, a metal plate 3 x 4 inches affixed to the right hand side of the above referenced "DANGER" sign having a red background with "DO NOT OPERATE BOOM ABOVE 80 DEGREES FROM THE HORIZONTAL PLANE" in 5/16 inch white letters
All forklifts shall have "NO RIDERS" in 2 inch black letters, stenciled vertically on both uprights of the fork frame facing the operator
When forklift upright size and/or hydraulic line placement will not accommodate placement of standard markings, local management will stencil "NO RIDERS" in minimum inch black letters at a location easily seen by the operator
When "WATCH STEP" legend conflicts with "HARNESS REQUIRED AREA" and associated yellow line marking, refer to Figure 2-7 for proper legend placement
The legend will be stenciled in 3 inch or 1-3/4 inch letters (depending on space availability) at approximately foot intervals beginning at each end of the rollerized bed and extending inward for 4 feet
3 Decal National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) Warning Label for 15 Passenger Vans
NHTSA has issued increased roll-over risk for 15 passenger vans with 10 or more occupants have three times the rollover ratio than those with fewer than 10 occupants
Any additional informational or instructional type decals deemed necessary, i
Decals installed inside vehicles may be removed if written approval is obtained from the base-level Safety Office and Wing Com- mander
Written approval for decal removal will be maintained in vehicle operations for reference purposes
Special markings prescribed herein are in addition to vehicle markings described in the preceding paragraphs unless otherwise specified
The sizes of the letters and numbers will comply with the specifications given in the remaining paragraphs of this chapter

context_relevancy.query: Short vs. long-term screwdriver storage
context_chunks:
Hold screwdriver as shown, with forefinger on rotating head (1)
Be sure the screwdriver fits the screw
If the screw is too large for the screwdriver tip, the tip will be damaged
5 CARE OF SCREWDRIVERS
When a screwdriver becomes nicked, or the edges become rounded, or when other damage occurs so that it does not fit screw slot, it can be reground or filed
Do not expose a screwdriver to excessive heat, as it may reduce the hardness of the blade
Replace a screwdriver that has a worn or damaged handle or rounded tip
After use, wipe screwdriver clean and place in rack or tool box
Screwdrivers used in the shop are best stored in a rack
This way the proper selection of the right screwdriver can be quickly made and fewer injuries will result
A poor fitting screwdriver will damage the screw head, slip off the screw, and cause personal injury
Use a screwdriver that has parallel sides and exactly fits the screw slot
Do not use a screwdriver as a chisel
3 SCREWDRIVERS SAFETY
Handle the screwdriver carefully
Do not carry a screwdriver in your pocket unless it has a pocket clip
Do not use a screwdriver near a live wire, to check a storage battery, or to determine if an electrical circuit is live
Do not hold the work in one hand while using the screwdriver with the other
If the screwdriver slips out of the slot, you will be most likely to put a gash in your hand
4 USING SCREWDRIVERS
The proper way to select and use a screwdriver is to always match the size of the screwdriver to the job and always match the type of screwdriver to the head of the screw
3 Keep Your Tool Set Complete If you are issued a tool box, each tool should be placed in it when not in use
This will help you to keep track of your tools
Avoid placing tools anywhere above machinery or electrical apparatus
A battered screwdriver may slip and spoil the screw slot or cause painful injury to the user
After use, return tools promptly to their proper places in the tool box
Improve your own efficiency by organizing your tools so that those used most frequently can be reached easily without sorting through the entire contents of the box
Avoid accumulating unnecessary items

1 | Common Screwdrivers | 22-1 |

5 | Offset Screwdrivers | 22-4 |

4 | Using an Offset Ratchet Screwdriver | 22-9 |

Jeweler's screwdrivers are made for driving and removing small size screws
Some jeweler's screwdrivers hav removable blades
A flexible screwdriver has a spring steel blade which bends, allowing the user to get around flanges, shoulders, and other part to drive and remove screws
10 Radio and Pocket Screwdrivers
A radio screwdriver (I) has a round blade that is 1-1/2 inches long
The pocket screwdriver (2) is also small, with a square blade that is 1-3/4 inches long
11 Screw Starter or Gimlet

context_relevancy.query: What is the minimum temperature required for Briner 2986 to cure in 1 hour as per the USAF technical manuals and orders?
context_chunks:
Briner 4765 or Briner 2905 shall be used as a primer for Briner 2986
Briner 2905 is two component, silver, heat resistant silicone coating
Apply Briner 2905 by conventional spray
Drying time is one hour (dust free), but will require 400 oF temperature before it cures completely
0 mil wet film thickness
Heat Resistant Silicone Coating-Briner 2986
Instructions for applying Briner 2986 are as follows:
Briner 2986 shall be used only as a topcoat for Briner 2905 or Briner 4765
Briner 2986 is a one component, black, heat resistant silicone coating
Apply Briner 2986 by brush, conventional spray, or airless spray
Briner 2986 will not cure without 350-400 oF for I hour
5 High Temperature Resistant Silicone Coating-Briner 4765
Briner 4765 is a two component, gray, high tempera ure resistant silicone coating
Because SAE AS5272 (MIL- PRF-46010), Type I must be cured at 400 F (205 C) for 1 hour and Type II must be cured at 300 F (1490 C) for 2 hours, they are not suitable for all applications as metallur- gical damage may occur at these temperatures
MIL- PRF-46147 and/or MIL-L-23398, lubricant, solid film, air cured, corrosion inhibiting are air cured, corrosion inhibit- ing, solid film lubricants that can be used on aluminum, steel and titanium in areas of infrequent operation and areas re- quiring long term protection under static conditions to pre- vent galling, corrosion, and metal seizure
MIL-L-23398, Type I (bulk) and Type II (aerosol) both require a 6 hour cure at a temperature of 77º +30 F (25 +20 C)
MIL-PRF-46147, Form 1 (bulk) and Form 2 (aerosol); Type I (standard solvents) require a 18 hour cure and Type II (low VOC content), both forms, re- quire a 24 hour cure at these same temperatures
Curing of both MIL-L-23398 and MIL-PRF-46147 solid film lubricants may be accelerated by allowing air drying at room tempera- ture for 30 minutes to flash off solvents and then heating to 1250 +50 F (520 +30 C) for 1 to 1 1/2 hours
Aircraft should not be operated until 72 hours has elapsed at a temperature of 75 oF (24 "C) or higher after the opcoat application is completed
If the temperature is less than 75 oF (24 *C) at any time during the cure cycle, it is prefer- ble to wait a full 7 days before flying the aircraft
Cure time before handling, taping, masking, etc
Time may be reduced to 4 hours if tempera- ture and humidity reach upper limit of 90 "F (32 *C) and 80 percent RH
Low temperature and low humidity of 60 "F (16 *C) and 30 percent RH retards cure rate to 12 hours minimum
Curing time before movement from controlled hangar environment at a temperature of75 oF (24 9C) or higher is 6 hours minimum after application of last paint coat
Curing time before decal application, 8 hours minimum
Curing time before engine run up, 30 hours minimum
Curing time before wet tape test, 48 hours minimum
Curing time before flight, 72 hours minimum
This paragraph shall be utilized for maintenance painting of ow speed aircraft with polyurethane paint systems
Maintenance painting of aircraft with maximum speeds of greater than 500 KIAS shall tilize Paragraph 6

Temperature and humidity requirements for maintenance painting of polyurethane systems are 75 oF (24 (C) and 50 percent RH and higher (not to exceed manufacturer's recommendations)
Lower temperatures and RH will require longer cure times
Curing time before flight, 24 hours minimum if temperature/humidity requirements are met
Maintenance painting cure times shall not be utilized when accomplishing an aircraft full paint
Maintenance painting of aircraft with maximum speeds of less than 500 KIAS shall utilize Paragraph 12
Curing time for maintenance painting related to complete leading edges (All), complete control surfaces (All), major skin panels 9 square feet (1296 square inches) and over, and engine intakes, 72 hours minimum
Curing time for maintenance painting related to skins panels less than 9 square feet (1296 square inches), fasteners, rivet heads, screw heads, and minor chipped and scratched areas less than 9 square feet (1296 square inches), 30 hours minimum

Aircraft should not be operated until 72 hours has elapsed at a temperature of 75 oF (24 "C) or higher after the opcoat application is completed
If the temperature is less than 75 oF (24 *C) at any time during the cure cycle, it is prefer- ble to wait a full 7 days before flying the aircraft
Cure time before handling, taping, masking, etc
Time may be reduced to 4 hours if tempera- ture and humidity reach upper limit of 90 "F (32 *C) and 80 percent RH
Low temperature and low humidity of 60 "F (16 *C) and 30 percent RH retards cure rate to 12 hours minimum
Curing time before movement from controlled hangar environment at a temperature of75 oF (24 9C) or higher is 6 hours minimum after application of last paint coat
Curing time before decal application, 8 hours minimum
Curing time before engine run up, 30 hours minimum
Curing time before wet tape test, 48 hours minimum
Curing time before flight, 72 hours minimum
This paragraph shall be utilized for maintenance painting of ow speed aircraft with polyurethane paint systems
Maintenance painting of aircraft with maximum speeds of greater than 500 KIAS shall tilize Paragraph 6
3 Heat Resistant Primer Briner 2905
Instructions for applying Briner 2905 are as follows:
Temperature and humidity requirements for maintenance painting of polyurethane systems are 75 oF (24 (C) and 50 percent RH and higher (not to exceed manufacturer's recommendations)
Lower temperatures and RH will require longer cure times
Curing time before flight, 24 hours minimum if temperature/humidity requirements are met
Maintenance painting cure times shall not be utilized when accomplishing an aircraft full paint
Maintenance painting of aircraft with maximum speeds of less than 500 KIAS shall utilize Paragraph 12

Curing time for maintenance painting related to complete leading edges (All), complete control surfaces (All), major skin panels 9 square feet (1296 square inches) and over, and engine intakes, 72 hours minimum

Curing time for maintenance painting related to skins panels less than 9 square feet (1296 square inches), fasteners, rivet heads, screw heads, and minor chipped and scratched areas less than 9 square feet (1296 square inches), 30 hours minimum
